prompt: What are the most effective approaches to scaling ion trap quantum computing from small-scale demonstration projects to large-scale systems capable of solving real-world problems? This research should investigate the various proposed scaling strategies, assess their feasibility, and evaluate which approaches are most likely to succeed based on current technological advancements and practical implementation challenges.
article:
Assumptions used (brief): “large-scale systems capable of solving real-world problems” means **fault-tolerant** trapped-ion quantum computers that can run **deep circuits** (≈ millions+ logical gates) with **useful logical-qubit counts** (from dozens to thousands, depending on application), not just NISQ-era demonstrations.

---

## 1) Where trapped-ion QC stands in 2024–2026 (and what that implies for scaling)

### 1.1 Demonstrated system scale + quality (selected, well-documented points)
- **Quantinuum H2-1 (QCCD, shuttling, all-to-all)**: commercial system upgraded to **56 trapped-ion qubits** while keeping mid-circuit measurement, qubit reuse, feed-forward, and all-to-all connectivity [[1]](https://www.hpcwire.com/off-the-wire/quantinuum-unveils-quantum-computer-featuring-56-trapped-ion-qubits-setting-new-performance-benchmarks/), [[2]](https://www.quantinuum.com/blog/quantinuums-h-series-hits-56-physical-qubits-that-are-all-to-all-connected-and-departs-the-era-of-classical-simulation). Quantinuum reports **random-circuit sampling** at 56 qubits with **XEB ≈ 0.35** (vs Google 2019 ~0.002), with public data and subsequent PRX publication trail [[2]](https://www.quantinuum.com/blog/quantinuums-h-series-hits-56-physical-qubits-that-are-all-to-all-connected-and-departs-the-era-of-classical-simulation), [[3]](https://github.com/CQCL/random-circuit-sampling), [[4]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.02501).  
- **Quantinuum H1-1**: reported **99.914(3)%** two-qubit gate fidelity (“three nines”) on a commercially available system, plus **Quantum Volume = 2²⁰** [[5]](https://www.quantinuum.com/blog/quantinuum-extends-its-significant-lead-in-quantum-computing-achieving-historic-milestones-for-hardware-fidelity-and-quantum-volume), [[6]](https://www.hpcwire.com/2024/04/16/quantinuum-reports-99-9-2-qubit-gate-fidelity-caps-eventful-2-months/).  
- **Quantinuum H2 (datasheet)**: typical error metrics for H2-class system: **1Q infidelity ~3×10⁻⁵**, **2Q infidelity ~10⁻³**, **SPAM ~10⁻³**, and **mid-circuit measurement crosstalk** reported as extremely small (typical 5×10⁻⁶) [[7]](https://docs.quantinuum.com/systems/data_sheets/Quantinuum%20H2%20Product%20Data%20Sheet.pdf), with documentation defining and measuring measurement/reset crosstalk [[8]](https://docs.quantinuum.com/systems/user_guide/hardware_user_guide/performance_validation.html).  
- **Quantinuum Helios (arXiv 2025)**: described as a **98-qubit trapped-ion quantum computer** with average infidelities reported (HTML excerpt) as **2.5(1)×10⁻⁵ (1Q)**, **7.9(2)×10⁻⁴ (2Q)**, **4.8(6)×10⁻⁴ (SPAM)**, using multiple operation zones (8 zones, batches up to 16 qubits) [[32]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.05465), [[33]](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.05465v1).  
- **IonQ Forte (Quantum journal 2024)**: benchmarked as an **all-to-all 30-qubit single chain** trapped-ion system; passes **Algorithmic Qubit (AQ) benchmarks up to AQ 29** and includes DRB across all 435 2-qubit pairs [[9]](https://quantum-journal.org/papers/q-2024-11-07-1516/), [[10]](https://quantumbenchmarkzoo.org/content/application-level-benchmark/protocols/algorithmic-qubit).  
- **Oxford Ionics / IonQ “electronic qubit control” line (2024–2025)**:
  - 2024 arXiv / 2025 PRX Quantum paper: “all-electronic control” in a multi-zone microfabricated trap; reports **single-qubit gate fidelity 99.99916(7)%** and **two-qubit maximally entangled states fidelity 99.97(1)%**, with low crosstalk and stable operation [[13]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.07694), [[104]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.07694v1).  
  - 2025 arXiv: **two-qubit gates with estimated error 8.4(7)×10⁻⁵ (>99.99% fidelity)** *without ground-state cooling*, plus robustness to higher phonon occupation (reported up to \bar{n} ≈ 9.4) [[14]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.17286). IonQ markets this as “four nines” 2Q fidelity and ties it to scaling claims [[15]](https://www.oxionics.com/announcements/ionq-achieves-landmark-result-setting-new-world-record-in-quantum-computing-performance/), [[16]](https://www.ionq.com/blog/accelerating-towards-fault-tolerance-unlocking-99-99-two-qubit-gate).  
  - SPAM record claim: **99.9993%** SPAM fidelity (Oxford Ionics announcement; linked arXiv) [[11]](https://www.oxionics.com/announcements/oxford-ionics-sets-new-world-record-in-qubit-readout/), [[12]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2409.05805).

### 1.2 What scaling is still blocked by (dominant bottlenecks)
Despite strong physical-qubit performance, moving from ~10–100 qubits to **useful fault-tolerant** machines is constrained by:

1) **Time-to-solution / throughput**
- In shuttling (QCCD) systems, *movement + cooling* can dominate runtime. IonQ highlights that in a canonical QCCD-style experiment, gate pulses were ~2% of runtime while **movement ~27% and cooling ~68%** (citing the seminal QCCD demonstration) and argues that eliminating ground-state cooling requirements can remove a major speed bottleneck [[16]](https://www.ionq.com/blog/accelerating-towards-fault-tolerance-unlocking-99-99-two-qubit-gate). The technical basis for high-fidelity gating without ground-state cooling is presented in the “smooth gate” preprint [[14]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.17286).  

2) **Wiring and control-plane scaling (“wiring problem”)**
- Quantinuum explicitly frames “wiring” as a totemic engineering challenge and points to broadcast / grid approaches as a solution direction [[5]](https://www.quantinuum.com/blog/quantinuum-extends-its-significant-lead-in-quantum-computing-achieving-historic-milestones-for-hardware-fidelity-and-quantum-volume), [[51]](https://www.quantinuum.com/press-releases/quantinuum-proves-their-quantum-computers-will-scale-with-major-hardware-innovation).  
- Electrode count often exceeds qubit count; a recent scaling analysis states a **rule-of-thumb ~10 electrodes per qubit**, motivating multiplexing and integration [[108]](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.01815v1).  

3) **Optical complexity (laser delivery, beam steering, stability, footprint, cost)**
- Trapped ions need multiple wavelengths for cooling, pumping, readout, and gates. IonQ explicitly positions **photonic integrated circuits** as a path to reduce bulk optics and raise qubit count/robustness [[17]](https://www.ionq.com/news/ionq-to-increase-performance-and-scale-of-quantum-computers-with-photonic). Quantinuum similarly discusses replacing free-space optics with integrated photonics for scaling [[37]](https://inspirehep.net/literature/3090166).  
- Integrated nanophotonics is promising but still difficult across multi-wavelength (often UV/visible) requirements and with low-loss switching/modulation at cryo.

4) **Motional heating / junction transport reliability in microfabricated surface traps**
- Heating scales strongly with ion-electrode distance (often near **d⁻⁴**), and cryogenic operation can reduce heating by ~**two orders of magnitude** in some measurements while preserving the d⁻⁴ scaling [[72]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1712.00188) (and earlier work shows ~10× reduction cooling from 300 K to ~150 K) [[70]](https://link.aps.org/doi/10.1103/PhysRevLett.97.103007).  
- Junction design and transport can be extremely robust (e.g., >10⁶ shuttles without loss in Y-junctions) but still add engineering complexity and can introduce motional excitation if not carefully optimized [[44]](https://iopscience.iop.org/article/10.1088/1367-2630/13/7/075018/pdf), [[64]](https://arxiv.org/abs/1210.3655).  

5) **Mid-circuit measurement/reset integrity and crosstalk**
- Essential for QEC. Quantinuum reports and measures mid-circuit measurement/reset crosstalk as a dedicated metric and quotes very small values on H2-class systems [[7]](https://docs.quantinuum.com/systems/data_sheets/Quantinuum%20H2%20Product%20Data%20Sheet.pdf), [[8]](https://docs.quantinuum.com/systems/user_guide/hardware_user_guide/performance_validation.html).  
- Independent work shows how difficult it is to measure/reset one ion without disturbing neighbors at micron-scale spacing, and demonstrates high “asset-qubit preservation” while performing destructive operations nearby [[34]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-50864-2).  

6) **Parallelism limits**
- Large advantage of ions is connectivity, but *parallel two-qubit gates* at scale are nontrivial (shared motional modes, optical resource contention, calibration). Architecture work emphasizes that parallel two-qubit operations can carry time or fidelity penalties and that current commercial QCCD platforms may restrict parallel 2Q gates within a trap for these reasons [[133]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.23519).

---

## 2) Scaling strategies: architectures proposed, how they work, and what is most feasible

The most credible scaling paths are **not mutually exclusive**—successful platforms likely combine:  
**(A) microfabricated multi-zone traps + (B) integrated optics/electronics + (C) QEC-first workflows + (D) eventually modular photonic networking.**

Below are the main strategies and an evidence-based feasibility assessment.

---

## 2.1 Scale “within one chain” (long linear crystals, no shuttling): simplest wiring, hardest physics

### Mechanism
Keep all qubits in one long chain (or a small number of long chains). Use shared motional modes for entangling gates (Mølmer–Sørensen style), relying on all-to-all coupling without moving ions.

### Advantages
- Minimal shuttling and junction complexity.
- Very high connectivity in principle.
- Fewer trap zones/hardware subsystems.

### Scaling limits (core issues)
- **Mode crowding**: as ion number grows, motional spectrum becomes dense; entangling gates become harder to address and may require longer pulses/complex optimal control. One proposal argues minimum achievable gate time tends to be set by smallest mode spacing, suggesting unfavorable scaling (at least ~N² gate-time growth in worst case) [[129]](https://arxiv.org/html/2311.01168v3).  
- **Heating in softened modes**: as N grows, low-frequency modes (e.g., axial COM) soften (frequency decreases) and are more susceptible to uniform electric-field noise; heating and instability can prevent high-fidelity gates [[129]](https://arxiv.org/html/2311.01168v3).  
- Quantinuum’s grid-transport work explicitly notes that long-chain approaches face “severe scaling obstacles,” including low axial frequencies hard to cool, radial-mode crowding, and gate times that scale with chain length [[50]](https://arxiv.org/html/2403.00756v1).

### Feasibility verdict
- **Good for near-term demos (10–50 qubits)** and some NISQ applications, but **unlikely to be the dominant route** to *very large* fault-tolerant systems unless paired with strong segmentation techniques (next section) or major breakthroughs in multi-mode gate compilation and heating suppression.

---

## 2.2 “Segmented long chain” via **dynamic optical potentials** (tweezers): promising concept, low maturity

### Mechanism
A proposal: keep a physically long chain, but **instantaneously segment it into cells** with dynamic optical potentials (e.g., optical tweezers) to control mode structure locally, enabling parallel operations in cells while preserving global connectivity via reconfiguration [[129]](https://arxiv.org/html/2311.01168v3).

### Claimed benefits (if realized)
- Heating and spectral issues depend on **cell size**, not total N [[129]](https://arxiv.org/html/2311.01168v3).  
- Parallel entangling operations across many cells [[129]](https://arxiv.org/html/2311.01168v3).  
- Potentially efficient mid-circuit measurement and connectivity via reconfigurable segmentation [[129]](https://arxiv.org/html/2311.01168v3).

### Practical challenges
- Requires high-power, ultra-stable optical systems with many beams; new calibration/control burdens.
- Must prove that segmentation itself doesn’t introduce decoherence, differential Stark shifts, or unacceptable technical noise.
- No clear system-level demonstrations at scale comparable to QCCD commercial systems (in the provided evidence set).

### Feasibility verdict
- **High-upside but speculative** relative to QCCD and modular networking. Watch as a research direction; not yet the “most likely” path to large-scale practical machines.

---

## 2.3 QCCD multi-zone shuttling (linear / racetrack / multi-zone): **most demonstrated route to scaling today**

### Mechanism
Use a microfabricated multi-zone trap: keep small crystals in zones optimized for gates, measurement, memory, loading, etc. Move ions (split/merge/shuttle/reorder) to bring desired qubits together for gates. This is the “quantum charge-coupled device” analogy [[41]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33828318/), [[132]](http://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2021Natur.592..209P/abstract).

### Why it scales better (in principle)
- Keeps entangling gates inside smaller crystals (better-controlled modes).
- Achieves **effective all-to-all connectivity** through rearrangement rather than long-range SWAP networks [[7]](https://docs.quantinuum.com/systems/data_sheets/Quantinuum%20H2%20Product%20Data%20Sheet.pdf).
- Spatial separation helps reduce measurement crosstalk and enables mid-circuit measurement/reset (critical for QEC) [[7]](https://docs.quantinuum.com/systems/data_sheets/Quantinuum%20H2%20Product%20Data%20Sheet.pdf), [[8]](https://docs.quantinuum.com/systems/user_guide/hardware_user_guide/performance_validation.html), [[41]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33828318/).

### Evidence it’s working (system-level)
- Full QCCD architecture integration demonstrated in a programmable system (Nature 2021), including parallel zones, fast transport, and mid-circuit measurement used for a teleported CNOT [[41]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33828318/), [[132]](http://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2021Natur.592..209P/abstract).  
- Commercial-scale systems with strong benchmarks:
  - H2-1: 56 qubits, mid-circuit measurement + reuse + feedforward, RCS XEB ≈ 0.35 [[2]](https://www.quantinuum.com/blog/quantinuums-h-series-hits-56-physical-qubits-that-are-all-to-all-connected-and-departs-the-era-of-classical-simulation), [[7]](https://docs.quantinuum.com/systems/data_sheets/Quantinuum%20H2%20Product%20Data%20Sheet.pdf).  
  - Helios: 98 qubits with reported average infidelities <10⁻³ for 2Q [[32]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.05465), [[33]](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.05465v1).  
- QEC demonstrations strongly suggest QCCD’s suitability for fault-tolerant primitives:
  - Better-than-physical logical qubits and repeated error correction cycles on H2 [[22]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.02280), [[84]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.02280).  
  - Fault-tolerant logical teleportation (transversal and lattice-surgery variants) with real-time decoding/corrections [[27]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.16728), [[28]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.16728).  
  - Logical magic state preparation via code switching with very low reported infidelity (≤5.1×10⁻⁴) [[85]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.14169).

### The two scaling bottlenecks for QCCD (and the best-known fixes)
**(1) Transport overhead (time + heating)**
- Movement and especially cooling can dominate time-to-solution [[16]](https://www.ionq.com/blog/accelerating-towards-fault-tolerance-unlocking-99-99-two-qubit-gate).  
- Fast shuttling with sub-quanta excitation is experimentally feasible with optimized waveforms (e.g., 210 μm in 6 μs with ~0.36 quanta gain in one demonstration) [[43]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41534-022-00579-3).  
- Junctions can be engineered for reliability and low barriers (e.g., pseudopotential barrier reduced from >1 eV to <2 meV in one Y-junction design) [[44]](https://iopscience.iop.org/article/10.1088/1367-2630/13/7/075018/pdf).  
- **Key fix direction**: reduce or eliminate slow cooling steps. The “smooth gate” result argues for high-fidelity gates without ground-state cooling, which would directly reduce QCCD schedule overhead [[14]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.17286), [[16]](https://www.ionq.com/blog/accelerating-towards-fault-tolerance-unlocking-99-99-two-qubit-gate).

**(2) Control scaling (electrodes + wiring + DAC count)**
- QCCD needs many electrodes; scaling analyses cite ~10 electrodes per qubit [[108]](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.01815v1).  
- Fix directions:
  - **On-chip / in-vacuum electronics**: integrated ±8 V DAC arrays with noise isolation via electrode isolation switches have been demonstrated at cryo, reducing external wiring (but requiring careful noise engineering and cryo variability management) [[107]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/120551/PhysRevApplied.11.024010.pdf).  
  - **Time-division multiplexed electrode drive**: proposes controlling ~10,000 electrodes using 104 high-speed DACs + 13 FPGAs (vs 10,000 DACs), with the caveat that voltage droop/recharge could affect motional stability and fidelity [[108]](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.01815v1).  
  - **Broadcast / co-wired electrodes** with small per-site digital selection (next section).

### Feasibility verdict
- **Highest near-term credibility** for scaling to **hundreds of physical qubits**, because it is already the basis of commercial systems approaching ~100 qubits with strong fidelities and mid-circuit capabilities [[7]](https://docs.quantinuum.com/systems/data_sheets/Quantinuum%20H2%20Product%20Data%20Sheet.pdf), [[33]](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.05465v1).  
- For **very large** systems, QCCD must (a) raise parallelism and (b) solve control-plane scaling and throughput (especially if QEC is the target).

---

## 2.4 2D grid-based surface-trap arrays with junctions + broadcast control: the most credible “next scaling step” beyond today’s QCCD

### Mechanism
Move from essentially 1D shuttling layouts to **2D grids of short segments connected by junctions** to reduce congestion and enable routing around traffic. Combine this with **broadcast/co-wired electrodes** and minimal per-site digital controls to address the wiring explosion [[50]](https://arxiv.org/html/2403.00756v1).

### Why 2D matters
- In linear-only architectures, ion chains can block each other; avoiding collisions requires slow reordering operations [[128]](https://www.cda.cit.tum.de/files/eda/2024_tcad_shuttling_for_scalable_trapped-ion_quantum_computers.pdf).  
- Junction-connected 2D topologies allow path diversity and reduce blocking [[128]](https://www.cda.cit.tum.de/files/eda/2024_tcad_shuttling_for_scalable_trapped-ion_quantum_computers.pdf).  

### Evidence (Quantinuum grid transport, 2024)
- A scalable control method in a **grid-based surface-electrode trap**, using co-wired electrodes plus a **site-wise binary control** enabling conditional operations with a fixed number of analog lines independent of grid size [[50]](https://arxiv.org/html/2403.00756v1).  
- Demonstrated primitives: conditional reorder within a site and conditional exchange between adjacent sites; measured **sub-quanta motional excitation** at **2.5 kHz exchange rates** averaged across multi-site region [[50]](https://arxiv.org/html/2403.00756v1).  
- Explicitly framed as solving both the **“wiring problem” and “sorting problem”** [[51]](https://www.quantinuum.com/press-releases/quantinuum-proves-their-quantum-computers-will-scale-with-major-hardware-innovation), [[50]](https://arxiv.org/html/2403.00756v1).

### What remains hard
- Large 2D arrays increase:
  - fabrication uniformity requirements (junction quality, stray fields),
  - calibration complexity (many zones),
  - need for fast, scalable, low-noise voltage generation and switching networks.
- Junction shuttling is often the time-dominant part of movement [[128]](https://www.cda.cit.tum.de/files/eda/2024_tcad_shuttling_for_scalable_trapped-ion_quantum_computers.pdf).

### Feasibility verdict
- **Very promising** because it directly addresses the two biggest QCCD scaling constraints: routing/sorting congestion and wiring.  
- Most likely path from ~100-qubit QCCD machines to **utility-scale physical-qubit fabrics** (hundreds to thousands), especially when co-designed with integrated electronics and photonics.

---

## 2.5 “All-electronic” trapped-ion control (microwave / near-field magnetic) + microfabrication: the most plausible path to *manufacturable* dense qubit fabrics

### Mechanism
Replace (most) laser-based coherent control with **chip-generated electromagnetic fields**:
- global/shared current-carrying traces generate fields,
- local tuning electrodes provide site selectivity (position/motional frequency tuning),
- lasers remain for cooling and readout (and possibly some specialized operations) [[104]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.07694v1).

### Evidence and why it matters
- All-electronic control paper demonstrates:
  - multi-zone chip (7 zones, up to 10 qubits),
  - **99.99916%** single-qubit gate fidelity,
  - **99.97%** entangled-state fidelity,
  - and argues for scalability with low crosstalk independent of device size [[13]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.07694), [[104]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.07694v1).  
- It provides a power dissipation estimate (**0.35 mW per “cell”** at 1 A for a representative trace geometry) and claims this could allow operation of **tens of thousands of qubits** in a 4 K cryostat (projection) [[104]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.07694v1).  
- The “smooth gate” result (high fidelity without ground-state cooling) also aligns strongly with the goal of high-throughput scalable fabrics [[14]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.17286).

### Key challenges
- Still requires extremely scalable and stable SPAM optics.
- Engineering high-current, low-noise cryogenic routing, packaging, and EM compatibility at scale is nontrivial.
- Demonstrations are at small qubit number; scaling claims are projections.

### Feasibility verdict
- Likely to be **one of the most important enabling technologies** for scaling, either:
  1) as the core control method for dense 2D fabrics, or  
  2) hybridized with integrated photonics for specific operations.  
- Strongly aligned with manufacturability (semiconductor processes) and wiring reduction compared to per-qubit laser addressing.

---

## 2.6 Modular/networked trapped-ion systems connected by photonic interconnects: essential for million-qubit scale, but link rates are the constraint

### Mechanism
Build many “small-to-medium” ion trap modules (each with high-quality local operations), then connect modules via **photon-mediated remote entanglement** (heralded Bell pairs), enabling distributed quantum computing and ultimately large-scale systems. IonQ’s scaling narrative explicitly relies on photonic interconnect milestones [[52]](https://www.ionq.com/blog/enabling-networked-quantum-computing-with-ion-photon-entanglement) and a modular roadmap [[38]](https://www.ionq.com/roadmap).

### What the best demonstrations say about feasibility (rates + fidelities)
- A concrete high-quality “elementary network” result reports **remote Bell pairs** with **94% fidelity** at an **average 182 s⁻¹** rate (success probability 2.18×10⁻⁴) between two trapped-ion nodes via photons and interference [[130]](https://ora.ox.ac.uk/objects/uuid:9e828ce6-e1a2-434f-9174-43ad8ba077ed).  
- High-fidelity time-bin photon entanglement (remote atoms mediated by time-bin photons) reports Bell-state fidelity **0.970(4)**, supporting time-bin encoding as a route to high-fidelity links [[57]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57557-4).  
- A “latency elimination” direction (“electron juggling”) argues that reducing classical and physical latencies could push REG rates to **>1,000 s⁻¹**, approaching an “atomic physics limit” (projection/goal) [[136]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.27005).

### Why it’s hard
- Remote entanglement is probabilistic; rates depend on photon collection efficiency, transmission loss, detector efficiency, and duty cycle/latency.  
- Even ~10²–10³ Bell pairs/s can become the dominant bottleneck if the architecture requires frequent inter-module operations or if large-scale QEC spans modules.

### Where modularity fits best
- **Late-stage scaling layer**: once single-module systems can support many logical qubits with high throughput, networking can connect “logical clusters” rather than raw physical qubits.  
- **Data-center scaling**: modularity avoids single-vacuum-chamber gigantism and enables manufacturing and serviceability.

### Feasibility verdict
- **Very likely required** for extremely large systems (tens/hundreds of thousands to millions of qubits), but **not sufficient alone**: the practical path is “strong local QEC + relatively sparse but high-quality interconnect.”

---

## 3) Cross-cutting enabling technologies that determine which scaling strategy wins

## 3.1 Microfabricated traps and manufacturability
Key manufacturability lessons:
- **CMOS-foundry traps** have been demonstrated in an unmodified 90 nm CMOS process, explicitly to leverage semiconductor scaling and enable co-integration with electronics/photonics [[60]](https://sclaser.mit.edu/documents/IonTrapCMOSAPLIAP_2014_KMehta_RRam.pdf), [[61]](https://www.osti.gov/biblio/22311366). The work argues heating levels could correspond to <10⁻⁴ error contribution in a 100 μs two-qubit gate [[60]](https://sclaser.mit.edu/documents/IonTrapCMOSAPLIAP_2014_KMehta_RRam.pdf).  
- Microfabrication constraints include thick dielectric layers to prevent breakdown (example guidance: >10 μm), stress control, UHV bake compatibility (~200 °C for ~2 weeks), and minimizing exposed dielectrics to reduce UV-induced charging [[62]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5614346/).  
- Overhang electrode designs and backside loading holes reduce dielectric charging and metal deposition/shorting risks [[63]](https://ar5iv.labs.arxiv.org/html/1008.0990).  
- Junction traps fabricated with VLSI methods can show reliable transport >10⁵ round trips and complex operations like splitting/merging chains [[64]](https://arxiv.org/abs/1210.3655).

**Implication:** scaling architectures that demand enormous zone counts will only be feasible if they converge on **high-yield foundry-like processes**, with robust surface treatment and packaging.

---

## 3.2 Heating and cryogenics: why most scaling roadmaps trend cold
- Distance scaling and temperature scaling of electric-field noise strongly motivate cryogenic operation: heating can be ~100× lower at 5 K than at room temperature in measured systems while maintaining ~d⁻⁴ scaling [[72]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1712.00188), and earlier work shows ~10× reduction cooling from 300 K to ~150 K [[70]](https://link.aps.org/doi/10.1103/PhysRevLett.97.103007).  
- Some reports suggest cryogenics may not always improve heating depending on surface conditions (a conflicting snippet exists) [[73]](https://link.aps.org/doi/10.1103/PhysRevA.92.020302), implying the “right answer” is likely **cryo + surface engineering/cleanliness** rather than cryo alone.

**Implication:** for dense 2D fabrics (small ion-surface distance), cryogenic operation is one of the most credible engineering levers to keep heating manageable.

---

## 3.3 Integrated photonics: the only believable way to scale laser delivery
Evidence that it can work:
- Nanophotonic waveguides + focusing grating couplers integrated into an ion trap chip addressed ions at 50 μm height; reported ~2 μm beam waist and quantified addressing crosstalk errors **10⁻² to 4×10⁻⁴** at 7.5–15 μm offsets [[66]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/111621/Ram_Integrated%20optical.pdf).  
- Direct fiber-coupled multi-channel integrated optics in cryo environments has been reported with entangled-state fidelities >99.3(2)% (abstract-level evidence) [[67]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33087915/).  
- Industry direction: IonQ + imec aim to move bulk optics into integrated photonic devices to reduce size/cost and increase qubit count [[17]](https://www.ionq.com/news/ionq-to-increase-performance-and-scale-of-quantum-computers-with-photonic). Quantinuum similarly positions integrated photonics as a long-term scaling enabler [[37]](https://inspirehep.net/literature/3090166).

What’s still missing at scale:
- Multi-wavelength integration across UV/visible for common ion species.
- Low-loss, high-extinction, fast switching/modulation integrated with traps.

A particularly relevant control building block:
- Piezo-optomechanical integrated Mach–Zehnder modulators show **38.7 dB extinction** and **<1 μs switching** and are discussed in the context of scaling trapped-ion control; reported single-qubit fidelities >99.7% (GST) using the modulated light [[103]](https://scispace.com/pdf/high-fidelity-trapped-ion-qubit-operations-with-scalable-292ek3zc.pdf). This is directly aligned with the needs of per-ion addressing at scale.

**Implication:** for any architecture that retains optical gates or relies on optical SPAM, **integrated photonics + integrated modulation** is a must-have for cost, stability, and footprint.

---

## 3.4 Scalable electronics for electrodes and real-time control
- **Cryogenic integrated HV CMOS DAC arrays** have been demonstrated, producing ±8 V electrode control with on-chip DACs and a mechanism (electrode isolation switch) to suppress noise by disconnecting the amplifier while holding electrode charge [[107]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/120551/PhysRevApplied.11.024010.pdf). This reduces external wiring but introduces cryo-variability and noise-management complexity [[107]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/120551/PhysRevApplied.11.024010.pdf).  
- **Time-division multiplexed electrode control** argues that thousands of electrodes could be driven by far fewer DACs (e.g., 10,000 electrodes with 104 high-speed DACs + 13 FPGAs) but flags fidelity risks from droop/recharge transients [[108]](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.01815v1).  
- Deterministic, low-latency timing stacks matter for QEC (mid-circuit measurement/feedback):
  - ARTIQ RTIO targets **nanosecond resolution** and **microsecond-scale latency**, but has scaling constraints like “lane” capacity and event ordering requirements [[118]](https://m-labs.hk/artiq/manual-beta/rtio.html), [[119]](https://m-labs.hk/artiq/manual/rtio.html).  
  - SoC-based control improvements can raise event throughput (benchmarked sustained event interval improvements down to ~376 ns in one Zynq architecture) [[120]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2111.15290).  
- **Automation and drift correction** is already practical and can materially improve operation: one work shows automated stray-field compensation boosting fluorescence proxy by **78% (gradient descent) / 96% (ML)** from a manually optimized baseline and recovering from UV-induced charging [[121]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-022-11142-7).

**Implication:** scaling ions is as much a **real-time systems engineering** problem as a physics problem; QEC will fail without fast, reliable, automated calibration and deterministic feedback control.

---

## 4) Fault tolerance: the scaling strategy must be “QEC-native,” not just “more qubits”

For real-world workloads, the winning approach must deliver:
1) **mid-circuit measurement/reset** with negligible crosstalk,  
2) high-fidelity 2Q gates (ultimately approaching 10⁻⁴ regime),  
3) high throughput QEC cycles (parallelism + low latency classical control), and  
4) an architecture that maps efficiently to a scalable code family.

### 4.1 Trapped-ion fault-tolerant primitives already demonstrated (strong evidence)
- **Better-than-physical logical qubits and repeated QEC** (Quantinuum H2): reports SPAM error 0.15%, 2Q error 0.14(1)%, and mid-circuit measurement/reset crosstalk ≤2×10⁻⁵ in the platform used for the demonstrations [[84]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.02280), with logical entangling error rates 9.8× to 800× lower than physical baselines depending on code and postselection choices [[22]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.02280), [[84]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.02280).  
- **Fault-tolerant logical teleportation** (Steane / color code) with real-time decoding/corrections: transversal teleportation process fidelity **0.975(2)**; lattice-surgery teleportation **0.851(9)**; Knill-style QEC-equivalent teleportation **0.989(2)** [[27]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.16728), [[28]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.16728).  
- **High-fidelity logical magic states via code switching**: prepares a Steane-encoded magic state with success probability **82.58%** and infidelity ≤ **5.1(2.7)×10⁻⁴**, reportedly below dominant physical 2Q and SPAM infidelities (≈10⁻³) [[85]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.14169).

**Why this matters for scaling strategy:** it strongly favors architectures that already support mid-circuit operations and flexible connectivity (QCCD-style systems have a lead here).

### 4.2 Code choices likely to matter specifically for ions
Trapped ions have unusual strengths (connectivity, long coherence) and weaknesses (measurement latency, complex control). This changes which codes are attractive:

- **Small color codes / Steane code**: good for early FT demonstrations due to transversal Clifford gates and relatively compact blocks; heavily used in trapped-ion FT experiments [[27]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.16728), [[28]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.16728), [[84]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.02280), [[85]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2506.14169).  
- **Surface codes**: the “default” industrial path, but mapping surface codes onto shuttling architectures requires careful co-design and may favor small trap capacities and grid-like layouts. A recent architecture study claims **two-ion traps** can be surprisingly near-optimal for surface-code cycles under certain wiring/topology assumptions, and highlights a power vs cycle-time bottleneck in wiring [[133]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.23519), [[134]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.23519).  
- **LDPC-type codes leveraging all-to-all connectivity**: an ion-chain noise model study suggests bivariate bicycle variants (BB5) could achieve the same logical error as a distance-7 surface code with ~**4× fewer physical qubits per logical qubit** at p=10⁻³ under its assumptions, though it also models the challenge of sequential unitaries and slow measurement (τm=30) [[90]](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.22071v3). This is a credible sign that ions may benefit disproportionately from **non-local, connectivity-hungry codes**.

**Implication:** the “most effective” scaling approach likely includes **code/architecture co-design**, not just hardware scaling. Ions may ultimately favor codes that exploit connectivity to reduce overhead, provided measurement and scheduling constraints are addressed.

---

## 5) Which scaling approaches are most likely to succeed (evidence-based synthesis)

### 5.1 Most likely near-to-mid term winner (2026–2029): **QCCD → 2D grid QCCD**, plus integrated electronics/optics
**Why:**
- It already exists at commercial scale with high fidelity and strong mid-circuit features [[7]](https://docs.quantinuum.com/systems/data_sheets/Quantinuum%20H2%20Product%20Data%20Sheet.pdf), [[33]](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.05465v1).  
- It has a concrete path to addressing wiring and sorting via grid-based broadcast control and junction networks [[50]](https://arxiv.org/html/2403.00756v1), [[51]](https://www.quantinuum.com/press-releases/quantinuum-proves-their-quantum-computers-will-scale-with-major-hardware-innovation).  
- It best matches the demonstrated QEC workflows on trapped ions (mid-circuit measurement/reset + feed-forward + reconfiguration) [[22]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.02280), [[28]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.16728), [[84]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.02280).

**Main risk:** throughput. QCCD must keep cycle times competitive by (a) reducing cooling overhead (potentially enabled by high-fidelity gating without ground-state cooling) [[14]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.17286), [[16]](https://www.ionq.com/blog/accelerating-towards-fault-tolerance-unlocking-99-99-two-qubit-gate), and (b) raising parallelism across many zones.

### 5.2 Most likely “manufacturable control” winner: **electronic qubit control + cryo + foundry traps**
**Why:**
- The all-electronic control results show extremely strong fidelities and explicitly argue for size-independent crosstalk and scalable power density [[104]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.07694v1).  
- Foundry manufacturing alignment has been demonstrated (CMOS traps) and is widely viewed as necessary for scale [[60]](https://sclaser.mit.edu/documents/IonTrapCMOSAPLIAP_2014_KMehta_RRam.pdf), [[61]](https://www.osti.gov/biblio/22311366).  
- Electronics integration (integrated DACs; multiplexing) directly attacks the control wiring explosion [[107]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/120551/PhysRevApplied.11.024010.pdf), [[108]](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.01815v1).

**Main risk:** translating small-zone demonstrations to large integrated fabrics while maintaining optical SPAM performance and controlling EM/thermal issues.

### 5.3 Most likely long-term scaling layer (post-2028): **modular photonic networking of logical clusters**
**Why:**
- Physical system sizes and vacuum/cryogenic constraints make monolithic million-qubit ion traps unlikely.  
- Remote entanglement has progressed to ~10² Bell pairs/s with high fidelity in elementary networks [[130]](https://ora.ox.ac.uk/objects/uuid:9e828ce6-e1a2-434f-9174-43ad8ba077ed), and there are plausible paths to >10³/s by latency elimination [[136]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.27005).  
- Industry roadmaps explicitly rely on modularity + photonic interconnect for very large targets [[38]](https://www.ionq.com/roadmap), [[52]](https://www.ionq.com/blog/enabling-networked-quantum-computing-with-ion-photon-entanglement).

**Main risk:** interconnect remains the bottleneck unless remote entanglement rates, multiplexing, and network scheduling improve substantially; networking raw physical qubits is far harder than networking logical qubits.

---

## 6) The most effective practical approach: a staged, co-designed scaling playbook

### Stage 1 (now → ~2027): “Quality-first scaling” to ~100–1000 physical qubits with QEC-native features
Most effective actions:
1) **Push 2Q error toward 10⁻⁴ while keeping system-wide uniformity**
   - Strong evidence that 10⁻⁴-level gates are becoming plausible in trapped ions (smooth-gate result) [[14]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.17286), and “three nines” is already routine commercially [[5]](https://www.quantinuum.com/blog/quantinuum-extends-its-significant-lead-in-quantum-computing-achieving-historic-milestones-for-hardware-fidelity-and-quantum-volume), [[7]](https://docs.quantinuum.com/systems/data_sheets/Quantinuum%20H2%20Product%20Data%20Sheet.pdf).  
2) **Eliminate ground-state cooling as a runtime requirement**
   - If gates remain high fidelity at Doppler temperatures, QCCD schedule overhead shrinks dramatically [[14]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.17286), [[16]](https://www.ionq.com/blog/accelerating-towards-fault-tolerance-unlocking-99-99-two-qubit-gate).  
3) **Demonstrate robust mid-circuit measurement/reset at scale**
   - Maintain extremely low measurement crosstalk across larger fabrics (Quantinuum-style quantified tracking is a good model) [[7]](https://docs.quantinuum.com/systems/data_sheets/Quantinuum%20H2%20Product%20Data%20Sheet.pdf), [[8]](https://docs.quantinuum.com/systems/user_guide/hardware_user_guide/performance_validation.html).  
4) **Adopt 2D routing + broadcast control primitives**
   - Expand from linear multi-zone to grid-based transport primitives that reduce wiring scaling and improve routing/sorting [[50]](https://arxiv.org/html/2403.00756v1).  
5) **System automation**
   - Invest heavily in automated compensation, calibration, and drift control (e.g., ML/optimization compensation) because manual tuning does not scale [[121]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-022-11142-7).

### Stage 2 (~2027 → ~2029): “Throughput-first fault tolerance” (tens to hundreds of logical qubits)
Most effective actions:
1) **Architecture/QEC co-design**
   - Decide whether surface code, color-code families, or LDPC-style codes best match the achieved error profile and measurement latency; ion-specific models show measurement slowness and sequential unitaries matter [[90]](https://arxiv.org/html/2503.22071v3).  
2) **Parallelism**
   - Expand operation-zone count and parallel gate capability (multiple zones already exist in commercial QCCD systems) [[7]](https://docs.quantinuum.com/systems/data_sheets/Quantinuum%20H2%20Product%20Data%20Sheet.pdf), [[33]](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.05465v1).  
3) **Integrated photonics for laser delivery + modulation**
   - Move away from bulk optics and toward waveguides/grating couplers and integrated switching/modulation (extinction >30 dB, ~1 μs switching is demonstrably feasible in integrated modulators) [[66]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/111621/Ram_Integrated%20optical.pdf), [[103]](https://scispace.com/pdf/high-fidelity-trapped-ion-qubit-operations-with-scalable-292ek3zc.pdf).  
4) **Electronics integration**
   - Combine broadcast control, integrated DACs, and/or multiplexing so electrode control does not scale linearly with qubits [[107]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/120551/PhysRevApplied.11.024010.pdf), [[108]](https://arxiv.org/html/2504.01815v1), [[50]](https://arxiv.org/html/2403.00756v1).

### Stage 3 (post-2028): “Networked scale”
Most effective actions:
1) **Network logical clusters, not raw qubits**
   - Use remote entanglement to connect modules that each maintain their own QEC cycles.  
2) **Increase REG (remote entanglement generation) rate and multiplexing**
   - Aim beyond ~10²/s (demonstrated) toward >10³/s (projected) while keeping fidelity high [[130]](https://ora.ox.ac.uk/objects/uuid:9e828ce6-e1a2-434f-9174-43ad8ba077ed), [[136]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.27005).  
3) **Integrated photonic collection + interference**
   - Treat photonic IO as a first-class scaling constraint (similar to integrated laser delivery).

---

## 7) Decisive validation metrics to watch (2026–2028)

To judge which approach is “most likely to succeed,” the following are the most discriminating system-level demonstrations (not just component fidelities):

1) **QEC cycle time + logical error rate under repeated cycles**
   - Not just a one-off logical state; repeated syndrome extraction with real-time decoding and quantified logical error per cycle (building on already-demonstrated repeated QEC) [[22]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.02280), [[84]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.02280), [[28]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.16728).  

2) **Demonstrated parallel two-qubit gate throughput**
   - Number of simultaneous 2Q gates at target fidelity across a large fabric, and how it scales with qubit count (architecture work suggests this is a key limiter) [[133]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.23519).  

3) **Transport cost per logical operation (QCCD / grid)**
   - Average shuttles/junction traversals per surface-code cycle (or per logical gate), and how much time is spent cooling vs gating. Claims that removing ground-state cooling is pivotal should be validated in full-stack runs [[14]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.17286), [[16]](https://www.ionq.com/blog/accelerating-towards-fault-tolerance-unlocking-99-99-two-qubit-gate).  

4) **Integrated photonics at multi-wavelength with low crosstalk**
   - Move from single-wavelength/small demos to multi-wavelength addressing across many sites with quantified crosstalk comparable to needed thresholds (integrated addressing already quantifies 10⁻² to 4×10⁻⁴ crosstalk levels) [[66]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/111621/Ram_Integrated%20optical.pdf).  

5) **Remote entanglement as an engineering primitive**
   - Stable high-fidelity Bell-pair generation at high rates over deployed fiber, with a roadmap to multiplexing and >10³/s attempts (benchmarks exist around 182/s at 94% fidelity) [[130]](https://ora.ox.ac.uk/objects/uuid:9e828ce6-e1a2-434f-9174-43ad8ba077ed), [[136]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.27005).

---

## Bottom line (most effective scaling approaches, ranked by current feasibility)

1) **QCCD multi-zone shuttling evolving into 2D grid-based QCCD fabrics**, because it has the strongest *end-to-end* evidence: commercial-scale qubit counts, excellent fidelities, mid-circuit measurement, and the most advanced trapped-ion fault-tolerant demonstrations [[2]](https://www.quantinuum.com/blog/quantinuums-h-series-hits-56-physical-qubits-that-are-all-to-all-connected-and-departs-the-era-of-classical-simulation), [[7]](https://docs.quantinuum.com/systems/data_sheets/Quantinuum%20H2%20Product%20Data%20Sheet.pdf), [[33]](https://arxiv.org/html/2511.05465v1), [[22]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.02280), [[28]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.16728), [[50]](https://arxiv.org/html/2403.00756v1).  
2) **Electronics-forward scaling (all-electronic gates + integrated control electronics + cryo + foundry fabrication)** as the most plausible way to make large trapped-ion systems manufacturable and controllable at scale, with early record fidelities and credible integration arguments [[104]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.07694v1), [[107]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/120551/PhysRevApplied.11.024010.pdf), [[60]](https://sclaser.mit.edu/documents/IonTrapCMOSAPLIAP_2014_KMehta_RRam.pdf).  
3) **Modular photonic networking** as the unavoidable long-term layer, but only after local modules can produce many logical qubits efficiently; current best demonstrated remote-entanglement rates/fidelities are promising but still a throughput constraint [[130]](https://ora.ox.ac.uk/objects/uuid:9e828ce6-e1a2-434f-9174-43ad8ba077ed), [[57]](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57557-4), [[136]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.27005).  
4) **Pure long-chain monolithic scaling without shuttling** is least likely to reach very large systems because of heating and mode crowding, unless segmentation-style concepts mature dramatically [[129]](https://arxiv.org/html/2311.01168v3), [[50]](https://arxiv.org/html/2403.00756v1).
